question.label: Landing check
question.name: What has been most on your mind in your first two weeks here, Riley?
question.description: Gives Riley room to set the agenda and surface what feels most present before narrowing in.

answer: I am comfortable reading the services, but I am less sure how decisions are really made here.
note: He feels comfortable reading the codebase but explicitly lacks clarity on how decisions get made here.

question.label: Decision-making map
question.name: When you say 'how decisions are really made' — what part is least clear?
question.description: Drills into the specific decision-making gap he named so the manager can remove that friction.

answer: I can trace most request paths now, though I still miss historical context behind certain tradeoffs.
note: He has a workable technical map but still lacks historical context behind tradeoffs, which is a mild remaining clarity gap.

question.label: Thread follow
question.name: trace most request — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
question.description: Runtime thread-follow injected because planner did not follow substantive answer.

answer: I have not been fully clear on who is on point for each domain when priorities conflict.
note: He names a specific ownership ambiguity during priority conflicts, which is a mild negative clarity signal; [THREAD-DEFERRED] ownership ambiguity in domain priority conflicts.

question.label: Ownership map
question.name: What feels clearest to you about what you own right now?
question.description: Checks whether he has a usable map of boundaries and decision space.

answer: I am trying not to interrupt people, so sometimes I keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking.
note: He is self-suppressing questions to avoid interrupting people, which is a strong signal that his ownership map is not yet usable; also hints at low-friction support needs.

question.label: Notes doc questions
question.name: What kinds of questions are sitting in that notes doc right now?
question.description: Turns the notes-doc habit into concrete unclear areas so the manager can remove friction fast.

answer: I would benefit from a clearer guide for what to escalate quickly versus what to batch.
note: Names a specific missing guide for escalation versus batching, which is a strong clarity gap and shows hesitation to engage directly.

question.label: Escalate vs batch
question.name: What would a useful escalate-versus-batch guide need to cover?
question.description: Drills into the specific escalation ambiguity he named so the manager can remove friction fast.

answer: The runbook helped, but some practical handoff details only show up during live incidents.
note: Names a concrete gap: practical incident handoff details are only learned live, signalling strong clarity friction and reduced confidence to engage; [THREAD-DEFERRED] practical handoff details during live incidents.

question.label: Context gaps
question.name: What context are you still waiting on before you change a design or make an architecture call?
question.description: Surfaces missing information that could quietly lead to rework or wrong assumptions.

answer: A short weekly architecture review with someone senior would help me calibrate faster.
note: Names a specific missing support — regular senior architecture review to calibrate faster — which is a strong blocker signal on clarity and wellbeing, with mild growth impact.

question.label: Useful next step
question.name: What would make the next two weeks feel steadier for you, Riley?
question.description: Closes on concrete support or adjustments that would improve his onboarding momentum.

answer: If I know exactly what ownership I should take this month, I can contribute with more confidence.
note: Names missing ownership clarity as what would make the next two weeks steadier, which is a mild negative signal on clarity and immediate sustainability.

question.label: Priority ranking
question.name: If you had to cut your work in half tomorrow, what would you drop first — and what's non-negotiable?
question.description: Forces a real priority ranking. Vague answers are a clarity signal.

answer: I will ask one architecture question in the next incident review instead of keeping it in my notes doc.
note: He named a specific next behavior change—asking an architecture question in the next incident review—which signals some priority clarity and initiative, though it answered action more than ranking.
